story:
Princess Antonia took the news with admirable composure: her brother, King Albrecht of Inner Alicia, had been arrested in another dimension for selling Shakespeare forgeries. It was the sort of impetuous scrape that suited him, and not entirely unexpected. She leafed through police photocopies of the handwritten pages and, with reluctant pride, observed that Albrecht’s spelling, grammar, and cadence were uncannily true to the Bard.

“How many?” she asked General Braunhaus, the solemn Chief of Intelligence. Four, he said: four whole plays composed, penned, and sold under Shakespeare’s name. A national emergency, he insisted. A king could not vanish for nine years only to reappear in a police cell On the Other Side of the Glass. Worse, the American officer—one McGriffin—reported that His Majesty might be facing a very long prison sentence.

Officer McGriffin, who had been “invited” through a mirror in a derelict New Jersey warehouse by polite but firm generals, suggested they come down to the station and speak with lawyers. The chandeliers of faerie-crystal and the Court’s velvet-and-high-heeled finery made him queasy about what would happen if he arrived with a full Cabinet in tow.

Antonia decided at once. As Albrecht’s sister and regent, who better to meet him on the Other Side? She rose from the throne, gathered her ministers, and set out across the Royal Gardens, laid out long ago by the Blue Wizard Arramor. On the walk, she began to explain for McGriffin’s benefit—only for him to say, without prompting, “You want to say that he was a Good King: fond of disguising himself among the peasantry, prone to rescuing foreign royalty from vile warlords, and brave enough to propose the abolition of serfdom.” Antonia, nonplussed, nodded and let him finish. His true fascination, though, was the world beyond the Glass. He sent ambassadors to fetch artefacts and knowledge. Automobiles impressed them—horseless carriages that whirred like magic—though the principle that made them go had eluded the court. “Gas,” McGriffin muttered, and she repeated it with clinical delight, a naturalist labeling a rare specimen.

Most of all, Albrecht loved literature. Inner Alicia had little of it—wizards discouraged books when there were dragons still to subdue, and elfin poetry was frankly doggerel. Your bards, she confessed, he loved. The dragon problem had dwindled anyway, after certain imported weapons made hunting them tragically efficient; she mentioned this coolly, without triumph.

They reached the Temple of the Glass, kept by three sorceresses who shared a single, rather grimy eye. Two slumbered; the third wrestled grit from the eyeball’s tender surface until she popped it back in and blinked. The Court Wizard Rundergast, portly and practical, confirmed the rush: “They’ve found the king.” Antonia explained her brother’s legal predicament and introduced McGriffin, who waved, uncertain whether “swain” was a compliment.

Yes, the officer had come through that morning with the Chief of Intelligence, the sorceress said. Did they all wish to go back now? Antonia brightened. She had always wanted to see New York. Was it true the society there existed in a low-level state of anarchy? They had no king? McGriffin admitted as much, while imagining with dread the American legal system attempting to process a monarch for art fraud.

“Never mind,” Antonia said kindly. Kings don’t vanish long—five hundred years at most, give or take a decade. Perhaps a sword in a stone would turn up. And with that, poised and curious, she stepped up to the Glass and crossed to the Other Side.
